senses.definition: to be the one who shows courage and does something risky that benefits all members of a group

examples.x: In my career, I've often been the one who bells the cat, taking on challenging projects others were hesitant to tackle.
The CEO suggested that the company needed a major overhaul, but no one wanted to be the one to bell the cat and implement the changes.
In the heated meeting, a junior employee volunteered to bell the cat by presenting a controversial idea to the management.
While the team recognized the need for cost-cutting measures, no one was willing to bell the cat and suggest employee layoffs.
Faced with a difficult and risky decision, she decided to be the one to bell the cat and address the long-standing issue in the department.

senses.definition: a risk or brave act taken without considering the consequences

examples.x: Starting a new business is a leap in the dark, as success is never guaranteed.
She decided to take a leap in the dark and move to a foreign country for a job opportunity.
Entering a new market with a unique product can be seen as a jump in the dark, but it has the potential for great rewards.
The decision to invest in a startup company was a risky jump in the dark, but it led to substantial financial gains.
In relationships, deciding to get married can often be a leap in the dark, as it involves an element of uncertainty about the future.

senses.definition: to continue to take risks because one has not faced any consequences so far

examples.x: Has Tara finally pushed her luck too far?
She's agreed to help on Saturday, but I think I'd be pushing my luck if I asked her to be here the whole weekend.

word_list.pronunciation: sˈeɪl klˈoʊs nˌɪɹ tə ðə wˈɪnd

senses.definition: to do something that is dangerous, improper, or possibly illegal

examples.x: His frequent late arrivals at work sail close to the wind of the company's punctuality policy.
The company's aggressive marketing tactics sometimes sail close to the wind of ethical boundaries.
When it comes to financial regulations, some investors prefer to sail close to the wind to maximize their profits.
The athlete's behavior on and off the field often sails close to the wind of sportsmanship and fair play.
In politics, some politicians choose to sail close to the wind of public opinion, even if it means taking controversial positions.

senses.definition: an action that is not beneficial to one in any way

examples.x: Betting all your money on a lottery ticket is often seen as a mug's game, as the odds of winning are extremely low.
Some people view online gambling as a mug's game, given the house's advantage and the potential for addiction.
Investing in a business venture with no solid plan is usually considered a mug's game, as it involves a high risk of financial loss.
Putting your life savings into speculative stocks can be seen as a mug's game, as it's highly unpredictable and volatile.
Falling for get-rich-quick schemes is a classic example of getting involved in a mug's game, as they often lead to financial losses.

senses.definition: to rely on chance in doing something

examples.x: I rolled the dice on a tool brand I haven't used before.
When he decided to quit his stable job and start his own business, he was rolling the dice on his financial future.
In relationships, you never know if it will work out, but sometimes you have to roll the dice and take a chance on love.
The company decided to roll the dice on a new advertising campaign to reach a broader audience.
When investing in the stock market, you're essentially rolling the dice, as market performance can be unpredictable.

senses.definition: to do something dangerous, risky, or foolish

examples.x: Texting while driving is a dangerous habit, akin to playing with fire on the road.
Disregarding financial advice and overspending is playing with fire, as it can result in debt and financial instability.
I don't want to see you play with fire when it comes to your health.
They have a tendency to play with fire by ignoring their obligations.
He often plays with fire by investing in risky stocks.

senses.definition: to behave in a way that is risky, dangerous, or reckless

examples.x: He must have a death wish, the way he drives at breakneck speeds on the highway.
Jumping off a cliff without a parachute is like having a death wish.
It's as if she has a death wish, constantly provoking dangerous animals in the wild.
Venturing into that abandoned, structurally unsound building shows that he must have a death wish.

senses.definition: to do something that is very likely to result in trouble or difficulty

examples.x: Driving without a seatbelt is like asking for trouble; it's dangerous and against the law.
Borrowing money from a loan shark is definitely asking for trouble due to the exorbitant interest rates.
Joining that contentious online debate and expressing strong opinions is a sure way to ask for trouble and confrontation with other users.

senses.definition: to knowingly do what can get one into trouble

examples.x: Unless you've got your stuff together then you're cruising for a bruising
Mocking your boss in front of the entire team is a surefire way to be cruising for a bruising at work.
Starting a fight with the biggest guy in the bar is like saying you're cruising for a bruising; it won't end well.

senses.definition: to have a life that involves one facing a lot of danger and taking great risks

examples.x: He's the kind of person who loves to live on the edge, constantly seeking new adrenaline-pumping adventures.
Their decision to quit their stable jobs and travel the world is a perfect example of living on the edge.
She lives on the edge by participating in extreme sports like skydiving and rock climbing.
Choosing a career in the entertainment industry is like living on the edge, with its unpredictable ups and downs.
Driving at such high speeds on icy roads is living on the edge; it's a recipe for disaster.
